english: offering of music Mil.: mčód-pa (mchod pa) sna-tsógs (sna tshogs) tógs-te (thogs te) carrying along with them all sorts of offerings Glr.; mčód-pai (mchod pa'i) kyád-par (khyad par) bču (bcu) the ten kinds of offerings Tar.; lha-mčód (lha mchod) offering or libation brought to a lha (lha); °bru-mčód ('bru mchod) an offering consisting of grain; dus-mčód (dus mchod) offerings presented at certain times Pth.; rgyun-mčód (rgyun mchod) daily offering; fig. dád-pai (dad pa'i) mčód-pa (mchod pa) Mil.; ytaṅ-rág-tu (gtang rag tu) sgrúb-pai (sgrub pa'i) mčód-pa (mchod pa) púl (phul) as a thanksgiving bring the offering of meditation! Mil. — Comp. mčód-kaṅ (mchod khang) house or place of offerings, of worship, Pth.; adopted as an appellation for the temple of the Jews, as lhá-kaṅ (lha khang) could not be used Chr. Prot. — mčód-kri (mchod khri) offering-table, Jewish altar, Chr. Prot. — mčód-lčóg (mchod lcog) prob. the same, C. — mčód-čá (mchod cha) Glr. = mčód-rdzás (mchod rdzas). — mčód-brjód (mchod brjod) words of adoration, doxology. — mčód-rtén (mchod rten) Ssk. चैत्य (religious building) and स्तूप (elevated place, elevation, tumulus) 1. etymologically; receptacle of offerings; 2. usually: a sacred pyramidal building, of a form varying in different countries and centuries, esp. near temples and convents, where often great numbers of these structures are to be seen. They were originally sepulchres, containing the relics of departed saints, and therefore called yduṅ-rtén (gdung rten); afterwards they were erected as cenotaphs, i. e. in honour of deceased saints buried elsewhere, but in more recent times they are looked upon as holy symbols of the Buddhist doctrine, v. Köpp. I, 533. — mčód-stégs (mchod stegs) offering-table, altar. — mčód-stód (mchod bstod) Sch.: an offering with a hymn of praise. — mčód-stón (mchod ston) an entertainment, as sort of libation, given to the priests Dzl.; perh. also a sacrificial feast. — mčód-sdóṅ (mchod sdong) 1. Sch. = mčód-rten (mchod rten) (?), 2. offering-lamp Sch., 3. the wick of such a lamp (in this sense it is used in a little botanical book). — mčód-gnás (mchod gnas) 1. prop. place where there is offered, place of sacrifice. 2. the object to which veneration is shown, image of a god Glr., sanctuary. 3. the offering priest, the sacrificator. — mčód-pa-po (mchod pa po) a sacrificer Cs. — mčód-°búl (mchod 'bul) the offering of a sacrifice Cs. — mčód-sbyín (mchod sbyin) id. (though elsewhere mčód-pa (mchod pa) sbst., as a gift to deities, is distinct from sbyín-pa (sbyin pa) a gift to men), also: sacrificer; mčód-sbyín-gyi (mchod sbyin gyi) °duṅ-kȧṅ ('dun khang) house where people assemble in order to perform sacrifices; sróg-gi (srog gi) mčód-sbyín (mchod sbyin) bloody offerings or sacrifices Tar. — mčód-mé (mchod me) offering-lamp, lighted in honour of a deity, and very common in the houses of Buddhists; *čod-mé (*chod me) púl-če* (phul ce*) W. to light such a lamp, (prop. to offer it). — mčód-rdzás (mchod rdzas), mčód-čá (mchod cha), mčód-pai (mchod pa'i) yo-byád (yo byad) instruments, utensils, requisite for festival processions in honour of a deity. — mčód-šóms (mchod shoms) or -bšáms (-bshams) the upper shelves in the holy repositories, containing the little statues of Buddha etc.

sometimes ṗyór-po (phyor po) 1. pretty, handsome, neat, elegant, ṗo-mčór-po (pho mchor po) a handsome man, bud-méd (bud med) mčór-mo (mchor mo) a pretty woman, esp. a smart gaily dressed female. — 2. W. also vain, conceited.
col. trodden, stamped; solid, firm, compact, like the Hindustani pakka.

I. pf. čag (chag) (s) 1. to break vb. n., snod (snod) čag-pa (chag pa) a broken vessel Dzl.; fig. ṅa-rgyál (nga rgyal) čag (chag) my pride is broken, frq.; der-°byón-stabs (der 'byon stabs) čag (chag) the opportunity of going there has been cut off Mil.; *lam čág-pa (lam chag pa) (also šog-pa) (shog pa)* C. a. a beaten, practicable road (a road broken through, v. °čég-pa) ('cheg pa) b. W. an impracticable, broken-up road. — 2. to be broken off, abated, beaten down from the price, rìn-čág-med-par (rin chag med par) there being no room for either asking or abating Mil. nt. — II. also čágs-pa (chags pa), pf. bčags (bcags), fut. bcag (bcag) (imp. °čog?) ('chog?) 1. to tread, to walk, to move, esp. when speaking respectfully or formally, yab (yab) més-kyi (mes kyi) žábs-kyis (zhabs kyis) bčágs-pai (bcags pa'i) sá-ča (sa cha) the place where my ancestors did walk Glr.; žabs (zhabs) čágs-pai (chags pa'i) ṗyag (phyag) ṗyir (phyir) gro (gro) follow me on my walk Mil. nt. — °čág-tu ('chag tu) or °čágs-su ('chags su) °gró-ba ('gro ba) to take a walk Dzl.; *°góm-čag-če* (*'gom chag ce*) W. to step along solemnly; čág-pėb-pa (chag peb pa) v. ṗyág-pėb-pa (phyag peb pa). — 2.
